coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dot implanted graphite carbon nitride nanorod with enhanced photocatalytic activity: Mechanisms, degradation pathway and DFT calculation
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Ying
Wang, Shuo
Sun, Zhiqiang
Liu, Qingliang
Ma, Jun
coredata.dc:description: Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is recognized as a promising photocatalyst for energy and environmental remediation. However, its performance has been limited by issues such as sluggish photocarrier transfer and inadequate light absorption. To address such challenges, we fabricated an innovative photocatalyst by implanting zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots (CQDs) within one-dimensional g-C3N4 nanorods. The CQD incorporation accelerated the charge separation/transfer, simultaneously, broadened the light-harvesting ability through the up-converted fluorescent properties. Density functional theory calculations were applied to gain insights into the influence of implanted CQDs on the band gap and charge behavior of CQDs/g-C3N4. Moreover, the π-π conjugation between the g-C3N4 and CQD enabled the photogeneration of charge carriers. As a result, the CQDs/g-C3N4 photocatalyst exhibited superb photocatalytic degradation under visible light irradiation, and the removal rates of tetracycline (TC) was 94.3%, surpassing that of pure g-C3N4 by 1.2 times. Additionally, CQDs/g-C3N4 processed excellent reusability and mineralization for TC degradation. The photocatalytic degradation pathway of TC was proposed using LC-MS. Mechanisms studies revealed that the photogenerated holes (h+), hydroxyl (•OH) and superoxide radicals (•O2 –) were the primary reactive species responsible for driving the photodegradation process. This study provided a valuable strategy for the design of efficient and stable photocatalysts for antibiotic wastewater remediation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalytic degradation
Tetracycline
Carbon quantum dots
Carbon nitride nanorods
DFT calculation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The research work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52200010 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project (No. 2022M710954 ), Open Project of State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment (Harbin Institute of Technology) (No. HC202240 ), Heilongjiang Provincial Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. LBH-Z22145 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectra, (b) XRD patterns of different catalysts. XPS spectra of CN and 4-CCN: (c) C 1s and (d) N 1s.
(a) SEM, (b) TEM, (c) HRTEM, (d) HAADF-STEM images, and (e–f) EDX mappings of 4-CCN.

(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra, (b) Tauc Plots obtained from the DRS analysis of the (αhν)2 vs. hν plots, (c) PL spectra, (d) transient photocurrent densities, (e) EIS spectra and (f) Solid-state EPR of different catalysts.
Band structure and density of states of (a. b) g-C3N4 and (c, d) CQDs/g-C3N4.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of TC upon visible light illumination over different catalysts and (b) the pseudo-first-order kinetic rate plot of TC degradation. Effect of (c) pH and (e) initial TC concentration on TC degradation over 4-CCN, and (d, f) the pseudo-first-order kinetic rate plot of TC degradation. Catalysts: 1 g/L, C 0[TC]: 20 mg/L (for a, c), initial pH of 7.0 (for a, e), T: 25 ± 2 °C.

(a) TOC removal during the photocatalytic degradation process of TC by CN and 4-CCN. (b) Liquid chromatography and (c) UV–vis spectra of photocatalytic degradation of TC using 4-CCN catalyst. (d) Possible TC degradation pathways. Catalysts: 1 g/L, C 0[TC]: 20 mg/L, initial pH of 7.0, T: 25 ± 2 °C.

(a) Effect of scavengers on the photocatalytic degradation of TC. (b) Cycling degradation of TC by the 4-CCN under visible light irradiation. Catalysts: 1 g/L, C 0[TC]: 20 mg/L, initial pH of 7.0, T: 25 ± 2 °C.
Possible mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of TC over CQD-g-C3N4.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dot implanted graphite carbon nitride nanorod with enhanced photocatalytic activity: Mechanisms, degradation pathway and DFT calculation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, 150090, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, 150090, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Marine Science and Technology
Harbin Institute of Technology at Weihai

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Marine Science and Technology, Harbin Institute of Technology at Weihai, Weihai, 264209, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Eco-Environmental Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100085, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Eco-Environmental Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100085, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is recognized as a promising photocatalyst for energy and environmental remediation. However, its performance has been limited by issues such as sluggish photocarrier transfer and inadequate light absorption. To address such challenges, we fabricated an innovative photocatalyst by implanting zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots (CQDs) within one-dimensional g-C3N4 nanorods. The CQD incorporation accelerated the charge separation/transfer, simultaneously, broadened the light-harvesting ability through the up-converted fluorescent properties. Density functional theory calculations were applied to gain insights into the influence of implanted CQDs on the band gap and charge behavior of CQDs/g-C3N4. Moreover, the π-π conjugation between the g-C3N4 and CQD enabled the photogeneration of charge carriers. As a result, the CQDs/g-C3N4 photocatalyst exhibited superb photocatalytic degradation under visible light irradiation, and the removal rates of tetracycline (TC) was 94.3%, surpassing that of pure g-C3N4 by 1.2 times. Additionally, CQDs/g-C3N4 processed excellent reusability and mineralization for TC degradation. The photocatalytic degradation pathway of TC was proposed using LC-MS. Mechanisms studies revealed that the photogenerated holes (h+), hydroxyl (•OH) and superoxide radicals (•O2 –) were the primary reactive species responsible for driving the photodegradation process. This study provided a valuable strategy for the design of efficient and stable photocatalysts for antibiotic wastewater remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics, as an emerging pollutant, are excessively used in medicine, agriculture, and aquaculture, a significant amount of refractory antibiotic wastewater is generated, posing a threat to the growth and development of plants, animal immunity, and even human health [1–4]. However, conventional treatment methods such as physical adsorption, biodegradation, and membrane filtration have limited effectiveness in treating antibiotic wastewater due to poor biodegradability [5,6]. Thus, there is an urgent need to develop an economical, efficient, and environmentally sustainable technology for treating antibiotic pollution in water environments.

Photocatalytic oxidation (PCO) effectively degrades various organic pollutants, making it a promising technology for wastewater treatment. Specifically, PCO can be selectively optimized for different organic pollutants and does not produce secondary pollutants, ensuring environmental safety [7,8]. Tetracycline (TC), as the second-highest antibiotic drug produced and used, plays an important role in the treatment of bacterial infections and the protection of human and animal health [9,10]. Based on this, developing new semiconductor photocatalysts for the efficient degradation of TC wastewater has become an important issue for relevant researchers, and the specific reaction mechanism of different types of photocatalysts still needs to be explored. Semiconductor materials commonly used for treating tetracycline (TC) wastewater include metal semiconductors such as TiO2, ZnO, and CdTe, which result in the waste of metal resources [11–13]. Non-metallic semiconductor carbon nitride (CN) may be an effective solution to avoid this deficiency. In addition, the unique structure and electronic properties of carbon nitride make it a promising material for tetracycline wastewater treatment. Recently, Zhao et al. developed Cd-doped carbon nitride photocatalysts for the photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline wastewater and investigated its degradation mechanism [14]. Wu et al. devised a novel approach to craft a carbon nitride material co-doped with Mg and O for the purpose of photo catalytically degrading tetracycline-contaminated wastewater. This dual decoration of Mg and O engendered an exceptional structural configuration capable of generating localized electrons around oxygen atoms, thereby augmenting the activation potential of reactants through the C→O←Mg pathway [15]. Although these photocatalysts have achieved good results in the degradation of tetracycline wastewater, there is a risk of secondary pollution caused by metal ion dissolution.

In this work, carbon nitride nanorods were prepared by a simple method, and then carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were introduced onto their surface to form CQDs/g-C3N4 (CCN) composite photocatalysts. The modification of carbon nitride nanorods by CQDs effectively regulated electron distribution on the material surface, which broadened the light absorption range of CN nanorods and enhanced the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [16]. In addition, the reasons for the high tetracycline degradation performance of CCN composites were elucidated through PL, TPR, EIS impedance spectroscopy, and LC-MS analysis of the final degradation products. The stability and recyclability of the composite catalyst were confirmed through four cycles of testing. Importantly, the main active species for the photocatalytic oxidation of organic pollutants in the CCN system were investigated.

All the raw chemicals were in analytical purity for preparation and testing. These chemicals were acquired from the Sinopharm and used without further purification. Its main materials were as follows: Melamine (C3N3(NH2)3), glacial acetic acid, deionized (DI) water, chitosan. Tetracycline (TC) was bought from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Initially, 30 g of melamine was combined with 60 mL of glacial acetic acid and 40 mL of water while continuously stirring due to the exothermic nature of the process. Stirring helps dissipate heat. Next, 20 g of melamine was heated in a covered crucible at 500 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 5.0 °C/min in air. Crucially, the sample must be poured into liquid nitrogen after removing the crucible from the muffle furnace at 500 °C. The g-C3N4 (CN) nanorods were obtained until liquid nitrogen vanished completely.

In a 100 mL beaker, a combination of 0.5 mmol of chitosan and 80 mL of deionized water was thoroughly blended and stirred for 30 min. The resulting mixture was subsequently transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave, sealed securely, and maintained at a temperature of 180 °C for a duration of 20 h. Afterward, the autoclave was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. The resulting CQDs were then subjected to a 48-h dialysis process within a dialysis bag, utilizing a cutoff molecular weight of 1000 Da, and subsequently stored under dark, low-temperature conditions at 4 °C.

To disperse 0.5 g of g-C3N4 nanorods in 10 mL of absolute ethanol, a certain volume (0.2, 0.4, 0.6 mL) of CQDs solution was added while vigorously stirring immediately. Then, 70 mL of water was added to the mixture and transferred to a hydrothermal reactor for catalyst synthesis at 180 °C. Afterward, the sample was washed three times with ethanol and water sequentially, dried. Finally, these CQDs/g-C3N4 materials were named 2-CCN, 4-CCN, and 6-CCN according to the amount of CQDs, respectively.

Details regarding the characterization equipment and associated parameters could be found in Text S1
(Supporting Information). The construction and refinement procedures for the Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculation models were outlined in Text S2 (Supporting Information).

Comprehensive information about the experimental setup and testing procedures for the degradation of tetracycline (TC) could be located in Text S3 (Supporting Information). It is important to note that all experiments were conducted after reaching an adsorption-desorption equilibrium before initiating the photocatalytic reaction.

FT-IR spectra were used to identify CQDs and their relationship with CN organic functional groups (Fig. 1 a). The sharp peak at 810 cm−1 indicated the presence of tri-s-triazine ring units of C-N-C modes. The characteristic peaks of CN surface related to C-N heterocyclic stretching vibration were lower compared to other literature [17,18] due to functional group destruction during quenching. Additionally, the broad peak at 3000-3700 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching mode of the marginal uncondensed amino group (NH/NH2) and hydroxyl group/adsorbed water [19,20]. It should be noted that the low content of CDQ did not cause significant changes in the structure or functional groups of CN to be present in FT-IR spectra. Therefore, we carried out subsequent characterization to further confirm the successful introduction of CQDs onto CN nanorods.

XRD patterns (Fig. 1b) were used to identify and determine the phase structures of the synthesized samples. The diffraction peaks of pure g-C3N4 at 13.5° and 27.5° were identified as (100) and (002) diffraction planes of graphite-like carbon nitride [21,22]. A leftward shift of the XRD diffraction peak from 13.5° in CN to 12.5° in all CCN indicated that CQDs altered the in-plane stacking of tri-s-triazine units in the CCN structure [23]. Moreover, a characteristic peak at 25.6° corresponding to CQDs was observed on the surface of the CCN sample, indicating the successful introduction of CQDs onto the surface of CN nanorods.

The XPS spectrum (Fig. S1) indicated that CN and CCN nanorods were composed of C, N, and a small amount of O, which could be from oxygen introduced during material preparation. For CN nanorods, the peaks of C 1s were located at 284.2, 287.6, and 293.3 eV, corresponding to C-C, N-CN, and π-π* bonds, respectively (Fig. 1c). Similarly, CCN nanorods exhibited three peaks at 284.3, 287.6, and 293.5 eV, attributed to C-C bonds, N-CN, and π-π* bonds, respectively (Fig. 1c) [24,25]. The lack of N elements in CQDs indicated that the electron transfer direction between CQDs and CN could be determined by the N 1s spectrum. In the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1d), CN exhibited peaks at 398.0, 398.9, 399.5, and 404.2 eV, which corresponded to sp2 hybridized nitrogen (C-NC), tertiary N in N-(C)3, amino groups (N-H), and N atoms of the graphitic aromatic triazine ring [26,27]. Moreover, treatment of CCN with CQDs caused a shift in the binding energy of N 1s to lower binding energy, suggesting the existence of electron transfer within the catalyst [28]. This transfer implied that the introduction of CQDs raised the surface electron density of CN material, which was advantageous in generating more •O2 - and enhancing photocatalytic efficiency.

SEM, TEM, and EDX elemental mapping were used to investigate the morphological structure evolution of 4-CCN nanorods. Fig. 2 a showed that 4-CCN nanorods had a rod-like structure with diameters ranging from 2 to 4 μm and lengths up to micrometers. The curling of the precursor due to sudden temperature drop during preparation was responsible for the formation of CN nanorods. Although CQDs were small in size, their presence on the surface of g-C3N4 was clearly visible in the TEM image of 4-CCN nanorods (Fig. 2b). Furthermore, Fig. 2c revealed that the size of the CQDs was approximately 1 nm. Additionally, when exposed to UV light, 4-CCN emitted a strong blue fluorescence at an emission wavelength of 254 nm (the inset of Fig. 2c). It indicated that the surface of 4-CCN was functionalized by CQDs. The uniform distribution of C and N elements covering the whole nanorods was confirmed by HAADF-STEM and EDX elemental mapping images (Fig. 2d–f) [29–32].

UV–vis DRS spectra were used to investigate the optical absorption properties of CN and CCN. The absorption edges of CN, 2-CCN, 4-CCN, and 6-CCN were observed at approximately 508, 525, 539, and 534 nm (Fig. 3 a–b), consistent with their intrinsic band gaps of 2.44, 2.36, 2.30, and 2.32 eV, respectively [33,34]. The band gaps of all photocatalysts were calculated using the following equation:
(1) αhν=(hν-Eg)1/2
where α is the light absorption coefficient, h is Planck's constant, ν is the light frequency, Eg is the band gap energy, and A is a constant. The CCN composite material exhibited a wider range of light absorption compared to CN, as illustrated in Fig. 3a. Furthermore, the incorporation of CQDs led to a reduction in the band gap of the composite material, as demonstrated in Fig. 3b. This introduction of CQDs resulted in an enhancement of the optical characteristics of CN, thanks to the surplus orbitals originating from sp2 carbon atoms. Additionally, CQDs served as efficient electron conduits, facilitating the rapid transfer of electrons from the composite material to reaction sites on its surface.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of photocatalysts were used to investigate charge separation and recombination processes as well as variations in surface states (Fig. 3c). Within the PL spectrum of CN, two significant emission peaks at 435 and 468 nm indicated electronic transitions involving sp3 C-Nσ and sp2 C-Nπ states. Notably, in the PL emission spectrum of the CCN catalyst, there was a shift from 468 to 470 nm, which was likely attributed to the presence of sub-interstitial defects introduced by the incorporation of CQDs. Additionally, the intensity of the PL characteristic peak of CCN catalyst was lower than that of CN, implying a longer electron-hole recombination time in the composite material. Notably, the photoelectron and hole recombination efficiency of 4-CCN nanorods was lower, suggesting better photocatalytic performance [35,36].

Transient photocurrent response (TPR) measurements revealed an elevation in the mobility of photo-carriers for CN, 2-CCN, 4-CCN, and 6-CCN under sunlight illumination (Fig. 3d). Among them, 4-CCN displayed the highest instantaneous photocurrent response, indicating better separation of e- and h+. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) Nyquist plots were conducted to investigate interfacial charge transfer processes for all samples (Fig. 3e), with a smaller semicircular shape representing lower electron transfer resistance values [37,38]. The smaller arc radius of 4-CCN indicated higher charge transfer efficiency due to the incorporation of CQDs. CQDs could provide an electron transport pathway on the catalyst, facilitating the progression of reactions. An appropriate amount of CQDs could be more favorable for efficient electron transfer, whereas an excessive load would result in overly complex electron transport pathways, electron competition, or limitations in electron transfer. In summary, an appropriate concentration of zero-dimensional CQDs effectively enhanced the photoelectrochemical conversion ability of CCN materials, whereas low or excessive amounts of CQDs limited the enhancement capability of CCN catalytic performance through ineffective contact between TC and catalyst [39,40].

The presence of unpaired electrons in a material could be detected by characteristic peaks in the solid electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra (Fig. 3f). These unpaired electrons exhibited apparent paramagnetism in a magnetic field, resulting in specific peaks in the EPR spectrum [41,42]. As the concentration of unpaired electrons increased, the strength of the EPR signal enhanced. Although no single electron was detected on the surface of CN material, a single electron signal peak was observed for CCN which could act as catalyst centers and participate in adsorption, reaction, and catalytic processes. Moreover, the EPR intensity of the 4-CCN was further amplified due to the π-π conjugation between the g-C3N4 and CQD, which enabled the photogeneration of charge carriers. Therefore, the introduction of CQDs could facilitate electron delocalization within the g-C3N4 structure, enhancing the concentration of unpaired electrons and electron mobility on the CN material surface.

DFT calculations were used to investigate the origin of the phenomenon where CQDs doping could reduce the band gap of g-C3N4 (Fig. 4 a–d). By optimizing the established model, it was found that the introduction of CQDs altered the planar structure of CN, consistent with XRD results. The band gaps of CN and CCN estimated by DFT calculations were 2.40 and 1.37 eV (Fig. 4a and c), respectively, which were approximately consistent with that of UV–vis DRS spectra (2.44 eV and 2.30 eV). However, impurity energy levels near the conduction band of CCN were observed within the bandgap, which might be attributed to the increased unpaired electrons by CQDs doping [43,44].

The discovery of unpaired electrons suggested that CQDs doping narrowed the band gap of CN and promoted electron transfer by generating impurity energy levels, thereby enhancing photocatalytic efficiency. The downward shift of the CB and VB indicated increased oxidizing ability of photogenerated holes in CCN, while the reducing ability of photogenerated electrons became weaker. As shown in Fig. 4b and d, the top of the CB and VB in both CN and CCN were mainly composed of C 2p and N 2p. The magnitude of DOS values represented the number of electronic states that could be accommodated in each energy level within a given energy range. At the Fermi level, the DOS value of CCN was significantly lower than that of CN, indicating that fewer electrons could be accommodated in its p-orbital, which was beneficial for accelerating electron transfer.

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms revealed increases in the specific surface areas of CCN composite photocatalysts relative to CN (Fig. S2). The specific surface area of the samples increased from 33.4 m2/g (CN) to 39.8 m2/g (4-CCN). The inset displayed the pore size distribution of CN and 4-CCN catalysts, where the pore size was mainly mesoporous with an average pore size of 2.16 and 2.58 nm, respectively. The introduction of CQDs increased both the specific surface area and pore size of the composite [45].

The photocatalytic oxidation performance of 4-CCN was evaluated under visible light irradiation and compared to nanorods made of CN and CCN with different ratios (Fig. 5 a). The TC degradation rates of CN, 2-CCN, 4-CCN, and 6-CCN nanorods were 78.2%, 90.8%, 94.3%, and 92.8%, respectively. However, significantly improved photocatalytic activity was observed for all CCN composites, showing steadily increasing TC degradation over time. Compared to CN, CCN exhibited higher photocatalytic activity, with the degradation rate of 4-CCN nanorods being about 1.2 times that of CN. Additionally, the Extended-Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetic model was used to fit the reaction rate process of photocatalytic oxidation of TC, with a high fitting degree [46,47]. The photocatalytic degradation rate of 4-CCN was the fastest, approximately 1.6 times that of CN (Fig. 5b), likely due to the load of rich CQDs enhancing the light adsorption and reducing recombination of photogenerated carriers [48]. However, excessive CQDs loading led to CQDs stacking and photon competition, thus weakening the photocatalytic effect of 6-CCN.

When the initial solution pH increased from 3.0 to 11.0, the degradation rates of TC by 4-CCN were 24.7%, 49.9%, 91.8%, 95.7%, and 63.4% in sequence (Fig. 5c). Likewise, k constant was the highest with the value of 0.020 min−1 when initial pH was 9.0 (Fig. 5d). The pH within the reaction system significantly influenced both the surface chemical state and the photocatalytic efficiency of the catalyst. Furthermore, TC manifested distinct forms at varying pH levels (TC+ at pH < 3.3, TC0 at 3.3 < pH < 7.7, and TC- at pH > 7.7), directly impacting its removal efficiency [49]. The pHZPC for 4-CCN was approximately 9.1. Consequently, at a pH of 3.0, TC existed as a cationic species (TC+), while the surface of 4-CCN was positively charged. This positive charge led to electrostatic repulsion between 4-CCN and TC+, resulting in reduced TC adsorption and a significant decrease in degradation efficiency. Moreover, at pH of 5.0 and 7.0, TC adopted an amphiphilic molecular state, inhibiting the electrostatic repulsion between TC and 4-CCN. This, in turn, enabled the catalyst to exhibit high catalytic activity. When the pH was approximately 9.0, the removal rate of TC reached its peak. This might be attributed to the initiation of photosensitive self-degradation of TC due to the transition from the π to π* state of the chromophore [50]. However, at a pH of 11.0, an excessive presence of OH– ions could occupy the adsorption sites on the photocatalyst, leading to a slight decrease in adsorption capacity [51]. Consequently, there was a slight reduction in the degradation rate of TC by 4-CCN at this pH level.

During the process of photocatalytic degradation of pollutants, the photocatalyst absorbed light energy and generated reactive oxygen species and other free radicals that oxidized and degraded the pollutants (Fig. 5e). As the TC concentration increased from 10 to 50 mg/L, the degradation rate of TC by 4-CCN showed a trend of first increasing and then decreasing. At initial TC concentrations of 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 mg/L, the corresponding degradation rates were 91.5%, 92.7%, 82.1%, 75.8% and 73.0%, respectively. It was observed that the effect of 4-CCN catalyst was better when the concentration of TC was less than 20 mg/L. Increasing the pollutant concentration results in more pollutant molecules in the reaction system, which enhanced contact between the active oxygen species and the pollutants, improving the efficiency of photocatalytic degradation. However, when the pollutant concentration was too high, it might lead to a complex reaction system, rapid reaction rates or competitive reactions, which could affect the efficiency of photocatalytic degradation [52]. The kinetic equation indicated that when the concentration of TC was 20 mg/L, the photocatalytic oxidation rate was the fastest (Fig. 5f).

The total organic carbon (TOC) content in water was measured to evaluate the degree of organic matter pollution and water quality status. The mineralization degree of TC by CN and CCN catalysts in the photocatalytic process was shown in Fig. 6 a. With increasing irradiation time, the TOC content in the solution gradually decreased. The mineralization degrees of pollutants by the CN and 4-CCN composite materials after the reaction were 43.1% and 62.1%, respectively. 4-CCN had higher practicality than CN due to its better mineralization performance. High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was used to measure the changes in intermediate products in the TC solution at different time intervals (Fig. 6b).

The characteristic peak of tetracycline at 6.2 min gradually decreased with increasing irradiation time, indicating the gradual decomposition of tetracycline into other products during the process. UV–vis spectra of the solution obtained at different times during the degradation of TC by 4-CCN nanorods were shown in Fig. 6c, where the characteristic peak of TC at 357 nm gradually decreased, indicating a decrease in the concentration of tetracycline in the solution. The decomposition rate of TC molecules was relatively fast at the initial stage of photocatalysis, but it decreased significantly as the reaction progressed, consistent with the degradation rate of photocatalysis. Therefore, the process of TC degradation by 4-CCN photocatalysis was analyzed by liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (Fig. 6d). To explore the potential degradation pathway of TC by 4-CCN, the degradation intermediates at different retention times were precisely analyzed by LC-MS/MS [53,54]. After irradiation for 30 min, some main intermediates with m/z of 445, 279, and 149 of TC were generated. This indicated that a large number of TC molecules were present in the solution during the initial stage of the reaction, while a small amount of TC molecules decomposed into other organic molecules. Then, the intermediates were further converted to smaller molecules (m/z = 415, 279, 149, 163, 256) by ring-opening reaction and dehydroxylation reaction. Finally, the molecular weights of the non-mineralized products were 279 and 149, respectively. The intermediate products in the remaining part were mineralized into CO2 and H2O, consistent with the results of TOC.

To study the effect of the main active species on TC degradation performance, a trapping agent experiment was carried out (Fig. 7 a). TEMPO, terephthalic acid, and formic acid were used as scavengers for trapping •O2 -, •OH, and h+, respectively. The additional number of quenching agents in the system had little effect on the catalytic performance. Except for TEMPO (50 mmol L−1), the additional amount of other quenching agents was 20 mmol L−1. The photocatalytic performance was reduced by 52.7%, 11.9%, and 11.2% with TEMPO, terephthalic acid, and formic acid, respectively. This indicated that •O2 - was the main reactive species during the degradation process, while •OH and h+ contributed little [55,56].

The photocatalyst 4-CCN could be rapidly separated from aqueous solution by suction filtration (Fig. 7b). To assess practical application, the regeneration performance and stability were discussed in detail. During the 4-cycle test, the degradation rates of TC by 4-CCN catalyst were 92.8%, 90.9%, 88.9%, and 87.8%. After four cycles of experiments, the degradation rate of 4-CCN was only reduced by 5%, demonstrating good stability of catalytic activity.

Mott-Schottky plot was carried out to investigate band-edge positions of CN and 4-CCN (Fig. 8 ). CN and 4-CCN were confirmed to be n-type semiconductors owing to the positive slopes of the linear plots. The flat band potential of catalyst was determined by the intersection of the curve and the x-axis. It is well accepted that the flat band edge positions is approximately 0.2 eV higher than conduction band edge n-type semiconductor [57]. Therefore, the conduction band value for the 4-CCN catalyst was −1.23 eV. By combining the bandgap value of 4-CCN (2.3 eV) calculated using UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 3b), the valence band of 4-CCN could be calculated with the value of +1.07 eV by subtracting the bandgap from the conduction band. After similar calculations, the conduction band and valence band values of CN were −1.19 eV and +1.25 eV, respectively.

Based on the findings presented above, the mechanism diagram for the photocatalytic degradation of TC solution using CQDs/g-C3N4 was shown in Fig. 9 . When light irradiated the surface of CQDs/g-C3N4, the photo-excited semiconductor generated e- and h+. These photo-excited e- and h+ were subsequently localized on the CB and VB of CQDs/g-C3N4 ( Eq. (2) ). The accumulated e- on the CB of CQDs/g-C3N4 participated in the reduction of O2 to form •O2 - due to the more negative CB potential of CQDs/g-C3N4 (−1.23 V vs. NHE) in comparison to the reduction potential of O2/•O2 – (−0.33 V vs. NHE), as shown in Eq. (3) . On the other hand, the VB of CQDs/g-C3N4 (+1.58 V vs. NHE) exhibited a lower oxidation potential compared to the oxidation potentials of OH–/•OH (+1.99 V vs. NHE) and H2O/•OH (+2.38 V vs. NHE). Consequently, h+ could not directly oxidize OH– and H2O to yield •OH, and •OH was produced through Eq. 4 and Eq. 5 [58]. Furthermore, h+ presented in the CQDs/g-C3N4 could directly oxidize TC molecules, resulting in the formation of smaller organic molecules. Moreover, the h+ of the CQDs/g-C3N4 could directly oxidize TC to small molecules. Subsequently, •O2 -, h+, and •OH combined with the pollutants in the solution to complete degradation ( Eq. (6) ) [59].
(2) CQDs/g-C3N4 + hν → h+ + e-
(3) O2 + e- → •O2 -
(4) O2 − + 2H+ + e− → H2O2
(5) H2O2 + e− → •OH + OH−
(6) h+ + •O2 - + •OH + TC → CO2 + H2O + intermediate

This work proposed a 0D CQDs implanted 1D g-C3N4 nanorod for efficient photocatalytic degradation of TC pollutants in water. The design protocol was proved to be effective in enhancing photocatalytic TC oxidation performance by CN, which was attributed to the enhanced TC adsorption, charge transfer, and broadening of the light absorption range. Notably, when CQDs were introduced into CN nanorods, it formed impurity levels due to the unpaired electrons, as EPR measurement and DFT calculation confirmed. Impurity levels served as capture or transport centers for photo-generated electrons and holes, which not only increases the amount of active species (h+, •OH, •O2 -) but also enhances the ability of the catalyst to absorb light. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency to TC of 4-CCN was 94.3%, which was higher than that of CN. 4-CCN exhibits excellent repeatable degradation ability and mineralization ability for TC. In summary, a practical, environmentally friendly, and non-secondary pollution photocatalyst has been developed that can be applied in the treatment of antibiotic wastewater in the future.

Ying Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology. Shuo Wang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Zhiqiang Sun: Visualization, Investigation. Qingliang Liu: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Jun Ma: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52200010), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project (No. 2022M710954), Open Project of State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment (Harbin Institute of Technology) (No. HC202240), Heilongjiang Provincial Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. LBH-Z22145).

ce:section.ce:para:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Designing 3D magnetic peony flower-like cobalt oxides/g-C3N4 dual Zscheme photocatalyst for remarkably enhanced sunlight driven photocatalytic redox activity

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Boron nitride quantum dots decorated ultrathin porous g-C3N4: intensified exciton dissociation and charge transfer for promoting visible-light-driven molecular oxygen activation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Yang, C. Zhang, D. Huang, G. Zeng, J. Huang, C. Lai, C. Zhou, W. Wang, H. Guo, W. Xue, R. Deng, M. Cheng, W. Xiong, Boron nitride quantum dots decorated ultrathin porous g-C3N4: intensified exciton dissociation and charge transfer for promoting visible-light-driven molecular oxygen activation, Appl. Catal. B: Environ. 245 (2019) 87-99.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design of porous double-shell Cu2O@CuCo2O4 Z-Scheme hollow microspheres with superior redox property for synergistic photocatalytic degradation of multi-pollutants

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zheng, X. Liu, L. Zhang, Design of porous double-shell Cu2O@CuCo2O4 Z-Scheme hollow microspheres with superior redox property for synergistic photocatalytic degradation of multi-pollutants, Chem. Eng. J. 389 (2020) 124339.
